description: Rules for microsite development ensuring shared resource usage and deployment prompts

rules.description: Sites can use Vite + React for frontend development (SPA, fast dev)

action.message:
Site frontend option 1: Vite 6.x + React 19.x
  Pros: Fast HMR, lightweight, ESM-first, simple deployment
  Best for: SPAs, admin dashboards, internal tools
  Note: Requires separate backend (FastAPI) for APIs

rules.rationale: Vite provides fastest dev experience for SPAs. Choose based on project needs.

examples.correct: Ask: 'Do you need: (1) SSR/SEO? (2) API endpoints? If yes to either → Next.js, if no to both → Vite+React'
examples.incorrect: Always default to one framework without considering needs

rules.description: Sites can use Next.js for frontend development (SSR, SEO, full-stack with API routes)

action.alternative: Vite + React for pure client SPAs without API needs
action.message:
Site frontend option 2: Next.js 15.x
  Pros: SSR/SSG, SEO-friendly, API routes (no separate backend needed), App Router
  Best for: Public sites, SEO-critical pages, sites needing simple APIs
  API routes: Great for CRUD, auth, webhooks without FastAPI overhead

rules.rationale: Next.js provides SSR, SEO benefits, and built-in API routes. Ideal when you need both frontend and simple backend in one project.

examples.correct: Ask: 'Do you need: (1) SSR/SEO? (2) API endpoints? If yes to either → Next.js, if no to both → Vite+React'
examples.incorrect: Always default to one framework without considering needs

examples.correct: Recommend Next.js for SSR and SEO benefits
examples.incorrect: Use client-only SPA for public marketing site

examples.scenario: User: 'Create a site with user authentication'
examples.correct: Recommend Next.js - API routes handle auth endpoints, no separate backend needed
examples.incorrect: Set up Vite+React plus separate FastAPI just for auth

examples.scenario: User: 'Build a dashboard with data fetching'
examples.correct: If simple CRUD → Next.js API routes. If complex ML/data processing → Vite+React + FastAPI
examples.incorrect: Always use FastAPI regardless of complexity

rules.description: Microsites with backend requirements should use FastAPI

action.alternative: Next.js API routes for simple endpoints
action.message: Microsite backend: Using FastAPI (auto OpenAPI docs, Pydantic validation, async support)

rules.rationale: FastAPI provides auto-generated OpenAPI docs, Pydantic validation, excellent async support, ideal for Python ML/AI endpoints

examples.scenario: User: 'Create a microsite with a data processing API'
examples.correct: Create FastAPI backend with Pydantic models, Vite+React frontend
examples.incorrect: Use Express.js or Flask without type validation

rules.description: When creating new UI components for microsites, prompt user to add reusable components to shared UI library

action.target: Add component to shared UI library

rules.rationale: Encourages code reuse across microsites, builds shared component library, reduces duplication

examples.scenario: Creating DataTable component for microsite A
examples.correct: Prompt: 'DataTable component created. Add to shared/ui-components for reuse in other microsites? [y/n/skip]'
examples.incorrect: Create component only in microsite A without offering to share

examples.correct: After implementation, ask: 'This Card component could be useful across microsites. Add to shared library?'
examples.incorrect: Silently keep component local to single microsite

rules.description: When microsite is ready for deployment, prompt user to publish to S3

action.message:
Microsite '{name}' is ready for deployment.

Publish to S3?
  [1] Public (permanent URL)
  [2] Temporary (presigned URL, 7 days)
  [3] Private (internal access only)
  [4] Skip deployment

rules.rationale: Ensures microsites are deployed for testing/review, centralizes hosting on S3, provides deployment options

examples.correct: Prompt: 'Build successful! Publish to S3? [1] Public [2] Temporary [3] Private [4] Skip'
examples.incorrect: Complete implementation without offering deployment

rules.description: Microsites must use existing domain models from shared/semantic/domains/
rules.rationale: Ensures consistency across microsites, leverages validated domain models, prevents duplicate definitions

examples.scenario: Microsite needs user authentication data model
examples.correct: Reference shared/semantic/domains/auth/ models (User, Session, Credential)
examples.incorrect: Create new User/Auth models in microsite

examples.correct: Import from shared/semantic/domains/finance/ (Transaction, Payment)
examples.incorrect: Define new Transaction type locally

rules.description: When microsite requires entities not in shared domain models, prompt user to extend shared models
rules.rationale: Grows shared domain model library organically, ensures new concepts are properly modeled and reusable

examples.correct: Prompt: 'ProductReview not in shared domains. Add to new product/ domain or keep local? [1] New domain [2] Local only'
examples.incorrect: Create ProductReview locally without offering to share

examples.correct: Check registry.yaml, find finance domain has Invoice, suggest using existing
examples.incorrect: Create duplicate Invoice model

rules.description: Microsites should use shadcn/ui for base UI components

action.alternative: Radix UI primitives for custom designs
action.message: UI components: Using shadcn/ui (Radix primitives + Tailwind, full customization)

rules.rationale: shadcn/ui provides accessible, customizable components that work well with Tailwind CSS

examples.scenario: Need a dropdown menu for microsite
examples.correct: Use shadcn/ui DropdownMenu component, customize with Tailwind
examples.incorrect: Build dropdown from scratch or use heavyweight library

rules.description: FastAPI backends must use Pydantic models for request/response validation

action.target: Pydantic models for all API contracts
action.message: FastAPI endpoints must use Pydantic models for validation. Check if model maps to shared domain.

rules.rationale: Pydantic provides automatic validation, serialization, and OpenAPI schema generation

examples.correct: Define CreateUserRequest(BaseModel) and UserResponse(BaseModel) Pydantic models
examples.incorrect: Accept raw dict without validation

rules.description: React components added to shared UI library must have Storybook stories

when: adding component to shared UI library

action.message:
Shared UI component '{component_name}' requires Storybook story.

Create {component_name}.stories.tsx with:
  - Default story
  - Variant stories (sizes, states)
  - Interactive controls

Storybook enables visual testing and documentation.

rules.rationale: Storybook provides visual documentation, enables isolated component development, facilitates design review and testing

examples.correct: Create Button.tsx AND Button.stories.tsx with Default, Primary, Secondary, Disabled stories
examples.incorrect: Create Button.tsx without Storybook stories

examples.correct: Include DataTable.stories.tsx showing empty state, loading, populated, sortable variants
examples.incorrect: Add DataTable without visual documentation

rules.description: Storybook stories should include default, variants, and edge case states

action.message:
Story file should include:
  1. Default - basic usage
  2. Variants - all visual variations
  3. States - loading, error, empty, disabled
  4. Sizes - if applicable
  5. Interactive - with args/controls

rules.rationale: Comprehensive stories serve as living documentation and catch visual regressions

examples.correct: Include: Default, WithFooter, Scrollable, Loading, Sizes (sm/md/lg), Closeable, NonCloseable
examples.incorrect: Single 'Default' story only

rules.description: All Python, Node.js, React, and Next.js apps must use the global semantic layer registry

action.target: Global semantic layer registry at shared/semantic/domains/
action.message:
App type detected: {app_type}

MUST use global semantic layer registry:
  Location: shared/semantic/domains/registry.yaml
  Available domains: core, auth, finance, workflow, email, etc.

Check registry before defining any data models.

rules.rationale: Ensures consistent data models across all applications, enables semantic interoperability, prevents duplicate definitions

examples.correct: Import models from shared/semantic/domains/. Use Pydantic models generated from domain schemas.
examples.incorrect: Define new User, Transaction, etc. models locally without checking registry

examples.scenario: Creating Next.js app with API routes
examples.correct: Use TypeScript types generated from shared/semantic/domains/ YAML schemas
examples.incorrect: Create ad-hoc TypeScript interfaces without referencing semantic layer

examples.correct: Reference shared/semantic/domains/registry.yaml for entity definitions
examples.incorrect: Invent new entity schemas without checking existing domains

rules.description: All React/UI applications must use the shared design system repository for components

action.target: Shared design system repository at shared/ui-components/
action.message:
React/UI application detected.

MUST use shared design system:
  Location: shared/ui-components/
  Base: shadcn/ui + Tailwind CSS
  Storybook: Required for all shared components

Check existing components before creating new ones.

rules.rationale: Ensures consistent UI/UX across applications, reduces duplication, enables design system evolution

examples.scenario: Creating React microsite with data table
examples.correct: Check shared/ui-components/ for existing DataTable. If not found, create and add to shared library.
examples.incorrect: Create DataTable locally without checking shared components

examples.correct: Import Button, Card, Modal from shared/ui-components/. Extend if needed.
examples.incorrect: Install separate component library or create from scratch

examples.correct: Configure to import from shared/ui-components/. Use existing primitives.
examples.incorrect: Duplicate component definitions from another project

rules.description: Follow structured workflow: create app → define needs → check registries → use or create with approval

action.message:
New {app_type} workflow:

1. CREATE: Initialize {app_type} structure
2. DEFINE: List required components and data models
3. CHECK: Audit registries:
   - UI: shared/ui-components/
   - Data: shared/semantic/domains/registry.yaml
4. USE/CREATE:
   - If found → import and use existing
   - If not found → create new, request human approval, add to registry

Proceeding with step 1...

rules.rationale: Structured workflow ensures registry usage, prevents duplication, maintains approval gates for new additions

examples.scenario: Creating new microsite for product catalog
examples.correct:
1. Create Vite+React project
2. Define: ProductCard, ProductGrid, Product entity
3. Check: ProductCard in ui-components? Product in domains?
4. Found ProductCard → use. Missing Product → create, get approval, add to domains/
examples.incorrect: Create all components and models locally without checking registries

examples.correct:
1. Define needs (charts, tables, metrics entity)
2. Check shared/ui-components for Chart, DataTable
3. Check domains for MetricEntity
4. Use existing, create missing with approval
examples.incorrect: Build everything from scratch without registry check

rules.description: New components or domain models not in registry require human approval before adding to shared libraries

when: creating new component not in registry
creating new domain model not in registry

rules.rationale: Human approval ensures quality gate for shared resources, prevents registry pollution

examples.correct: Check ui-components → not found → 'UserAvatar not in registry. Approve adding? [y/n/local]' → if y, create and add with Storybook
examples.incorrect: Create UserAvatar locally and forget about sharing

examples.correct: Check domains registry → not found → 'Subscription entity not in registry. Add to billing domain? [y/n/local]' → if y, add to YAML with validation
examples.incorrect: Define Subscription inline without registry consideration

rules.description: Before creating new UI components, audit shared design system for existing components
rules.rationale: Prevents component duplication, encourages reuse and extension over creation

examples.correct: Check shared/ui-components/DropdownMenu, if exists use it. Otherwise add shadcn/ui DropdownMenu to shared.
examples.incorrect: Build custom dropdown without checking existing options

rules.description: Always try to use known primitives (Artifact, Workflow, Rule, Guardrail, Validation) before creating new concepts

action.target: Known primitives from shared domain model
action.message:
Before creating new entities, check for these known primitives:

  Core Primitives:
    - Artifact: Any generated output (document, code, presentation)
    - Workflow: State machine with states, transitions, triggers
    - Rule: AI steering behavior definition
    - Guardrail: Tech/architecture preference constraint
    - Validation: Schema or constraint check

  Also check:
    - Entity, Action, Intent, Observer
    - Phase, Transition, Trigger

  Location: shared/semantic/domains/

  Using existing primitives ensures consistency and interoperability.

rules.rationale: Known primitives provide semantic consistency across systems, enable cross-project interoperability, and reduce concept duplication

examples.correct: Use Artifact entity from shared domains for generated documents
examples.incorrect: Create new 'GeneratedDocument' entity without checking primitives

examples.correct: Use Workflow, Transition, and Rule primitives from shared domains
examples.incorrect: Create custom 'ApprovalFlow', 'ApprovalStep' entities

examples.correct: Use Guardrail entity and Rule schema from shared domains
examples.incorrect: Define inline constraint format without checking existing

examples.correct: Use Validation entity pattern from shared domains, possibly Observer rules
examples.incorrect: Create new 'InputChecker' concept

rules.description: Core primitives (Artifact, Workflow, Rule, Guardrail, Validation) must be defined in shared domain models

action.message:
Core primitive '{primitive_name}' not found in shared domains.

Required primitives that MUST exist in shared/semantic/domains/:
  - core/: Entity, Identifier, AuditInfo
  - artifact-library/: Artifact, ArtifactType, GenerationPrompt
  - workflow/: WorkflowDefinition, StepDefinition, TransitionDefinition
  - observer/: Rule, Violation, CheckResult
  - guardrails/ (proposed): Guardrail, TechPreference, ArchitecturePattern

Add '{primitive_name}' to appropriate domain before proceeding.

rules.rationale: Core primitives form the semantic foundation of the system and must be centrally defined for consistency

examples.scenario: Guardrail entity not in shared domains
examples.correct: Create shared/semantic/domains/guardrails/schema.yaml with Guardrail entity definition
examples.incorrect: Define Guardrail locally in the project only

examples.correct: Add Validation entity to observer domain or create validation domain
examples.incorrect: Create ad-hoc validation schema in project

rules.description: TypeScript applications must use strongly-typed models generated from shared domain models

action.message:
TypeScript detected.

MUST use strongly-typed models:
  1. Check shared/semantic/domains/ for existing type definitions
  2. Generate TypeScript interfaces from YAML schemas
  3. Never use 'any' for domain entities
  4. Use Zod or similar for runtime validation

Example generation:
  shared/semantic/domains/core/user.yaml → types/User.ts

rules.rationale: Strong typing ensures type safety, IDE support, and catches errors at compile time. Domain models provide semantic consistency across the codebase.

examples.scenario: Creating a Next.js app with user management
examples.correct: Generate User, Session interfaces from shared/semantic/domains/auth/. Use these types throughout the app.
examples.incorrect: Define ad-hoc User interface or use 'any' type

examples.correct: Import Transaction type from generated types (sourced from domains/finance/). Use strict typing.
examples.incorrect: Create loose object types or use 'as any' casts

examples.correct: Use type definitions that match backend Pydantic models, both generated from shared domains
examples.incorrect: Manually duplicate type definitions without shared source of truth

rules.description: Python applications must use Pydantic models generated from shared domain models

action.message:
Python application detected.

MUST use Pydantic models:
  1. Check shared/semantic/domains/ for existing model definitions
  2. Generate Pydantic models from YAML schemas
  3. Use BaseModel for all domain entities
  4. Include field validators where appropriate
  5. Use model_config for serialization settings

Example generation:
  shared/semantic/domains/core/user.yaml → models/user.py (Pydantic)

rules.rationale: Pydantic provides runtime validation, serialization, and automatic OpenAPI schema generation. Shared domain models ensure consistency across Python apps.

examples.scenario: Creating FastAPI backend for user service
examples.correct: Generate User, UserCreate, UserResponse Pydantic models from shared/semantic/domains/auth/
examples.incorrect: Use plain dict or dataclass without validation

examples.scenario: Building Python CLI tool with config
examples.correct: Use Pydantic Settings or BaseModel for config, types from shared domains
examples.incorrect: Load JSON into unvalidated dict

examples.correct: Define Pydantic models for input/output schemas sourced from domain definitions
examples.incorrect: Pass untyped dicts between pipeline stages

rules.description: When creating or completing HTML files, always offer to publish to S3

action.message:
HTML file created: {filename}

Publish to S3 for easy sharing?
  [1] Public (permanent URL)
  [2] Temporary (presigned URL, 7 days)
  [3] Private (internal access only)
  [4] Skip

Script: prototypes/proto-s3-publish-vayfd-023/s3_publish.py

rules.rationale: HTML files are often intended for sharing. S3 publishing provides easy access URLs and consistent hosting.

examples.correct: After creating chart.html, prompt: 'Publish to S3? [1] Public [2] Temp [3] Private [4] Skip'
examples.incorrect: Create HTML without offering sharing options

examples.correct: After generating report.html, offer S3 publish with presigned URL option
examples.incorrect: Leave report in local filesystem only

examples.correct: Upon build completion, prompt for S3 deployment with public URL option
examples.incorrect: Complete build without deployment prompt

ZIP archive created: {filename}

Publish to S3 for easy sharing?
  [1] Public (permanent URL)
  [2] Temporary (presigned URL, 7 days)
  [3] Private (internal access only)
  [4] Skip

Script: prototypes/proto-s3-publish-vayfd-023/s3_publish.py

rules.rationale: ZIP archives are often created for distribution. S3 publishing provides easy download URLs.

examples.correct: After creating dist.zip, prompt: 'Publish to S3? [1] Public [2] Temp [3] Private [4] Skip'
examples.incorrect: Create ZIP without offering sharing options

Publish to S3 for easy sharing?

Script: prototypes/proto-s3-publish-vayfd-023/s3_publish.py
